    <div class="comment-body markdown-body js-comment-body soft-wrap user-select-contain d-block">
      <blockquote>
<p dir="auto">This is a nice personal workflow, but the hype is way ahead of the evidence.</p>
<p dir="auto">There is no benchmark, no task definition, no scale curve, and no comparison against serious baselines. We do not know whether this is better than hybrid RAG, BM25 plus reranking, vector search, GraphRAG, hierarchical summaries, long-context prompting, NotebookLM, Perplexity Spaces, or ChatGPT Projects. Calling it a new architecture without that evidence is premature.</p>
<p dir="auto">The core problem is that an LLM Wiki is lossy compression. You take raw documents and rewrite them into derived wiki pages. That may be useful for a small curated corpus, but it can also drop caveats, dates, minority views, exact wording, edge cases, and source context. Once people start querying the wiki instead of the original material, summary errors become part of the knowledge base.</p>
<p dir="auto">Updates are also not solved. Adding one new source can affect many entity pages, concept pages, timelines, summaries, and indexes. At scale, this becomes graph maintenance: detecting what changed, resolving conflicts, avoiding duplicates, preserving provenance, preventing stale claims, and not silently breaking old pages. “Ask the LLM to maintain it” is not an engineering solution unless there are validators, source hashes, span-level citations, regression tests, and human review.</p>
<p dir="auto">It also does not remove retrieval. Once the wiki grows beyond a modest size, you still need search, ranking, indexing, reranking, chunking, and access control. At that point the markdown wiki is just another indexed corpus, not a replacement for RAG.</p>
<p dir="auto">The production issues are mostly ignored: permissions, multi-user edits, audit logs, rollback, deletion, sensitive data, source versioning, concurrency, compliance, cost, latency, and update frequency. These are not small details; they are exactly where knowledge-base systems fail.</p>
<p dir="auto">So the reasonable claim is narrow: this can be a useful workflow for small-to-medium, slow-moving, human-curated research folders. It is much less convincing for large, fast-changing, high-stakes, multi-user, or enterprise knowledge bases.</p>
<p dir="auto">The idea is fine. The framing is the problem. Without benchmarks, baselines, provenance guarantees, update-evaluation tests, and clear boundary conditions, “LLM Wiki” is mostly a good name for a familiar pattern, not proof that RAG is obsolete.</p>
</blockquote>
<p dir="auto">We ran exactly these comparisons -- BERT F1, token economics, cross RAG comparison -- <a href="https://github.com/Yarmoluk/ckg-benchmark/blob/main/paper/main.pdf">https://github.com/Yarmoluk/ckg-benchmark/blob/main/paper/main.pdf</a></p>
    </div>
  </task-lists>
  
</div>

  <clipboard-copy role="menuitem" value="The schema observation is underrated. The difference between an LLM that maintains a wiki and one that just answers questions in a wiki-shaped directory is almost entirely that file.

I ran into the same consistency problem across projects (not just wikis) and ended up building scaffy (https://github.com/ColonelPanicX/scaffy), which is a dead simple python script that bootstraps the schema layer for any project: collaboration contract, session protocols, kanban, agent profiles, etc.

The idea is that every project gets the same guardrails so the LLM isn't relearning the rules each session. Works well as a starting point for the setup that Karpathy described here.

    <div class="comment-body markdown-body js-comment-body soft-wrap user-select-contain d-block">
      <p dir="auto">The schema observation is underrated. The difference between an LLM that maintains a wiki and one that just answers questions in a wiki-shaped directory is almost entirely that file.</p>
<p dir="auto">I ran into the same consistency problem across projects (not just wikis) and ended up building scaffy (<a href="https://github.com/ColonelPanicX/scaffy">https://github.com/ColonelPanicX/scaffy</a>), which is a dead simple python script that bootstraps the schema layer for any project: collaboration contract, session protocols, kanban, agent profiles, etc.</p>
<p dir="auto">The idea is that every project gets the same guardrails so the LLM isn't relearning the rules each session. Works well as a starting point for the setup that Karpathy described here.</p>

  <clipboard-copy role="menuitem" value="Hi Andrej,

Your LLM Wiki gist (https://gist.github.com/karpathy/442a6bf555914893e9891c11519de94f) became the foundation of our project — **NEXUS**, a multi-agent memory system running on a VPS with 6 AI agents.

## What we built

NEXUS implements your 3-layer pattern:
- **Raw sources** — immutable source documents (32 files)
- **Wiki** — LLM-generated markdown pages (entities, concepts, sources)
- **Schema** — CLAUDE.md / SOUL.md files that define agent identity and workflows

## Stack
- 6 AI agents on VPS
- Weaviate (vector DB) + Ollama (local LLM) + Wiki.js
- GraphRAG with typed schema (Source, Concept, Agent, Project)
- Skills system, session logging, compression with OpenRouter
- MCP servers: Brave Search, Tavily, YouTube, Apify, MiniMax, Slack, qmd

## The key insight that changed everything for us

> "The wiki is a persistent, compounding artifact. The cross-references are already there. The contradictions have already been flagged. The synthesis already reflects everything you've read."

This transformed how we think about agent memory. Instead of RAG re-deriving context every time, we compound knowledge session over session.

## Gratitude

Thank you for sharing this pattern. The idea of "wiki as a persistent, compounding artifact" instead of another RAG layer that re-derives context every time — this was the insight that changed everything for us.

We saw your gist on May 1, 2026. By May 5, we had NEXUS running in production with 6 agents, GraphRAG, and full memory persistence.

The ecosystem you inspired is impressive: Kompl, SwarmVault, Aura, llmwiki-cli, ΩmegaWiki, Link. Every one of them took the same pattern and made it their own.

Thank you for planting the seed

> "The wiki is a persistent, compounding artifact. The cross-references are already there. The contradictions have already been flagged. The synthesis already reflects everything you've read."

Luciano - https://github.com/lrdeoliveira/nexus-ai-memory" data-view-component="true" class="dropdown-item btn-link">

      <p dir="auto">Hi Andrej,</p>
<p dir="auto">Your LLM Wiki gist (<a href="https://gist.github.com/karpathy/442a6bf555914893e9891c11519de94f">https://gist.github.com/karpathy/442a6bf555914893e9891c11519de94f</a>) became the foundation of our project — <strong>NEXUS</strong>, a multi-agent memory system running on a VPS with 6 AI agents.</p>
<h2 dir="auto">What we built</h2>
<p dir="auto">NEXUS implements your 3-layer pattern:</p>
<ul dir="auto">
<li><strong>Raw sources</strong> — immutable source documents (32 files)</li>
<li><strong>Wiki</strong> — LLM-generated markdown pages (entities, concepts, sources)</li>
<li><strong>Schema</strong> — CLAUDE.md / SOUL.md files that define agent identity and workflows</li>
</ul>
<h2 dir="auto">Stack</h2>
<ul dir="auto">
<li>6 AI agents on VPS</li>
<li>Weaviate (vector DB) + Ollama (local LLM) + Wiki.js</li>
<li>GraphRAG with typed schema (Source, Concept, Agent, Project)</li>
<li>Skills system, session logging, compression with OpenRouter</li>
<li>MCP servers: Brave Search, Tavily, YouTube, Apify, MiniMax, Slack, qmd</li>
</ul>
<h2 dir="auto">The key insight that changed everything for us</h2>
<blockquote>
<p dir="auto">"The wiki is a persistent, compounding artifact. The cross-references are already there. The contradictions have already been flagged. The synthesis already reflects everything you've read."</p>
</blockquote>
<p dir="auto">This transformed how we think about agent memory. Instead of RAG re-deriving context every time, we compound knowledge session over session.</p>
<h2 dir="auto">Gratitude</h2>
<p dir="auto">Thank you for sharing this pattern. The idea of "wiki as a persistent, compounding artifact" instead of another RAG layer that re-derives context every time — this was the insight that changed everything for us.</p>
<p dir="auto">We saw your gist on May 1, 2026. By May 5, we had NEXUS running in production with 6 agents, GraphRAG, and full memory persistence.</p>
<p dir="auto">The ecosystem you inspired is impressive: Kompl, SwarmVault, Aura, llmwiki-cli, ΩmegaWiki, Link. Every one of them took the same pattern and made it their own.</p>
<p dir="auto">Thank you for planting the seed</p>
<blockquote>
<p dir="auto">"The wiki is a persistent, compounding artifact. The cross-references are already there. The contradictions have already been flagged. The synthesis already reflects everything you've read."</p>
</blockquote>
<p dir="auto">Luciano - <a href="https://github.com/lrdeoliveira/nexus-ai-memory">https://github.com/lrdeoliveira/nexus-ai-memory</a></p>

  <clipboard-copy role="menuitem" value="For larger Markdown wikis, line-anchored local retrieval before edits becomes a real bottleneck: agents need to read the exact lines they're about to touch, not whole files.

I built SeekLink for that loop: index a local `.md` vault, search semantically, then fetch a specific `PATH:LINE` window.

```bash
seeklink index --vault ./wiki
seeklink search "why SQLite for search?" --vault ./wiki --json
seeklink get decisions/search.md:42 -C 20 --vault ./wiki   # 20 lines of context
```

Plain subprocess interface, JSON stdout, local-only.

https://github.com/simonsysun/seeklink" data-view-component="true" class="dropdown-item btn-link">
      
                Copy Markdown

    <div class="comment-body markdown-body js-comment-body soft-wrap user-select-contain d-block">
      <p dir="auto">For larger Markdown wikis, line-anchored local retrieval before edits becomes a real bottleneck: agents need to read the exact lines they're about to touch, not whole files.</p>
<p dir="auto">I built SeekLink for that loop: index a local <code class="notranslate">.md</code> vault, search semantically, then fetch a specific <code class="notranslate">PATH:LINE</code> window.</p>
<div class="highlight highlight-source-shell" dir="auto"><pre class="notranslate">seeklink index --vault ./wiki
seeklink search <span class="pl-s"><span class="pl-pds">"</span>why SQLite for search?<span class="pl-pds">"</span></span> --vault ./wiki --json
seeklink get decisions/search.md:42 -C 20 --vault ./wiki   <span class="pl-c"><span class="pl-c">#</span> 20 lines of context</span></pre></div>
<p dir="auto">Plain subprocess interface, JSON stdout, local-only.</p>
<p dir="auto"><a href="https://github.com/simonsysun/seeklink">https://github.com/simonsysun/seeklink</a></p>
    </div>
  </task-lists>
  
</div>

  <clipboard-copy role="menuitem" value="Huge milestone from this thread: `llmwiki` just crossed 1K GitHub stars (and 100 forks)!
<img width="866" height="53" alt="Screenshot 2026-05-05 at 2 50 47 PM" src="https://gist.github.com/user-attachments/assets/59a69307-6af6-47b2-83fb-7922ebc19b74" />


I built it because Andrej's LLM Wiki gist felt immediately useful, but I wanted the idea to be boringly concrete... a local CLI, plain files, immutable sources, generated markdown, provenance, linting, exports, and agent access.

The project now has:

- `compile --review` so generated pages can be approved or rejected before landing
- claim-level provenance like `^[paper.md:42-58]`
- typed schema/page kinds and seed pages
- confidence and contradiction metadata
- image/PDF/transcript ingest
- chunked retrieval with BM25 reranking
- `llms.txt`, JSON-LD, GraphML, and Marp exports
- MCP tools
- Claude/Codex/Cursor session ingest

The part that still feels most useful to me is how the knowledge builds up over time... add sources, improve the wiki, ask better questions, save better answers, and the next agent starts fully informed instead of another empty chat window.

The roadmap is now focused on the parts that make this real at scale... local web UI, graph/context packs, evals, and rollback/audit.

Thanks to everyone who has tried it, filed issues, opened PRs, or posted competing implementations here. This thread has been our best product research.

https://github.com/atomicmemory/llm-wiki-compiler" data-view-component="true" class="dropdown-item btn-link">
      
                Copy Markdown

</clipboard-copy>  <div aria-live="polite" aria-atomic="true" class="sr-only" data-clipboard-copy-feedback></div>

    <div class="comment-body markdown-body js-comment-body soft-wrap user-select-contain d-block">
      <p dir="auto">Huge milestone from this thread: <code class="notranslate">llmwiki</code> just crossed 1K GitHub stars (and 100 forks)!<br>
<a target="_blank" rel="noopener noreferrer" href="https://private-user-images.githubusercontent.com/1934146/587966620-59a69307-6af6-47b2-83fb-7922ebc19b74.png?jwt=eyJ0eXAiOiJKV1QiLCJhbGciOiJIUzI1NiJ9..2s97jZ6KSRAiqh7yMAgmE6AcjSKv7tq4l_9ieM5PIMQ"><img width="866" height="53" alt="Screenshot 2026-05-05 at 2 50 47 PM" src="https://private-user-images.githubusercontent.com/1934146/587966620-59a69307-6af6-47b2-83fb-7922ebc19b74.png?jwt=eyJ0eXAiOiJKV1QiLCJhbGciOiJIUzI1NiJ9..2s97jZ6KSRAiqh7yMAgmE6AcjSKv7tq4l_9ieM5PIMQ" style="max-width: 100%; height: auto; max-height: 53px;; aspect-ratio: 866 / 53; background-color: var(--bgColor-muted); border-radius: 6px; display: block" class="js-gh-image-fallback"></a></p>
<p dir="auto">I built it because Andrej's LLM Wiki gist felt immediately useful, but I wanted the idea to be boringly concrete... a local CLI, plain files, immutable sources, generated markdown, provenance, linting, exports, and agent access.</p>
<p dir="auto">The project now has:</p>
<ul dir="auto">
<li><code class="notranslate">compile --review</code> so generated pages can be approved or rejected before landing</li>
<li>claim-level provenance like <code class="notranslate">^[paper.md:42-58]</code></li>
<li>typed schema/page kinds and seed pages</li>
<li>confidence and contradiction metadata</li>
<li>image/PDF/transcript ingest</li>
<li>chunked retrieval with BM25 reranking</li>
<li><code class="notranslate">llms.txt</code>, JSON-LD, GraphML, and Marp exports</li>
<li>MCP tools</li>
<li>Claude/Codex/Cursor session ingest</li>
</ul>
<p dir="auto">The part that still feels most useful to me is how the knowledge builds up over time... add sources, improve the wiki, ask better questions, save better answers, and the next agent starts fully informed instead of another empty chat window.</p>
<p dir="auto">The roadmap is now focused on the parts that make this real at scale... local web UI, graph/context packs, evals, and rollback/audit.</p>
<p dir="auto">Thanks to everyone who has tried it, filed issues, opened PRs, or posted competing implementations here. This thread has been our best product research.</p>
<p dir="auto"><a href="https://github.com/atomicmemory/llm-wiki-compiler">https://github.com/atomicmemory/llm-wiki-compiler</a></p>
    </div>
  </task-lists>
  
</div>
